text: A Spacemaker II GSI balloon was inserted into the retroperitoneal space just above the anterior superior iliac spine.
Here we see the balloon being inflated under direct vision to create the space.
This is the retroperitoneal space after the balloon has been removed.
Here we are inserting a 2 millimeter trocar superior and posterior to our camera port.
A 5mm trocar is now being inserted midway between our camera port and our 2mm port.
Here we are dissecting just above the psoas muscle with a 5mm Kittner.
Now we can begin to see the inferior vena cava to the left of the screen.
Now we see a two millimeter grasper being used to begin the dissection of the inferior vena cava.
While dissecting along the vena cava, bleeding may occur from adjacent lymph nodes.
Here the bleeding is controlled with the two millimeter grasper.
Clips are sometimes necessary to control the small perilymphatic vessels.
Manipulation of the vena cava with the two millimeter grasper is to be avoided as much as possible.
Here we see a structure that looks like the sympathetic chain.
However, this is another lymph node chain.
We use intraoperative x-ray to verify the appropriate level of the spine from which to remove the sympathetic ganglion.
We can begin to see the sympathetic chain coming into view.
The sympathetic chain is being cut at the L4, L5 level.
The sympathetic chain was removed down to the S1 level.
Generally speaking, removal of the L4 S1 segment is sufficient for patients with reflex sympathetic dystrophy of the lower leg.
The sympathetic chain was removed through the 5mm port and sent to pathology for verification.
